1:
"Wherefore seeing we also are compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight, and the sin which doth so easily beset us, and let us run with patience the race that is set before us," The witnesses note the call to run the race, laying aside every weight. On "compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses," the grammatical voices note the spectators. Barnes and Robertson note the image drawn from the heroes of faith (ch. 11): "compassed about (surrounded, as in an amphitheatre) with so great a cloud (nephos — a vast multitude) of witnesses (martyron)" — the saints of old surround us like a great host; "witnesses" meaning either spectators watching the race, or those who by their lives bore witness to faith.

On "let us lay aside every weight, and the sin which doth so easily beset us," the witnesses note the runner's stripping. Barnes and Calvin note the figure of the athlete: "lay aside every weight (onkon — every encumbrance, anything that hinders, as a runner strips off all bulk) and the sin which doth so easily beset us (euperistaton — the sin that clings closely, easily entangles)" — the believer is to cast off every hindrance and the clinging sin that would trip him.

On "run with patience the race that is set before us," the puritan Henry notes the perseverance: "run with patience (hypomone — endurance) the race (agona — contest) that is set before us" — the Christian life is a race requiring patient endurance to the end; we run the appointed course steadily. The verse displays the call to run the race, laying aside every weight.

The witnesses agree the substance: surrounded by the great cloud of the faithful (ch. 11), the believer is to strip off every encumbrance and the clinging sin, and run with patient endurance the appointed race (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry). The verse displays the race. The verse marks the cloud: of witnesses. The verse displays the laying aside: every weight. The verse displays the sin: easily besetting. The verse displays the running: with patience. The image displays the runner stripped, surrounded by the faithful host. The verse displays the witnesses: surrounding. The verse displays the weight: cast off. The verse displays the race: set before us. The call to run the race, laying aside every weight, displays the great exhortation flowing from the roll of faith: surrounded by so vast a cloud of witnesses, let us strip off every weight and the sin that so easily entangles, and run with patient endurance the race set before us. The verse displays the race, run by looking unto Jesus (v.2).
2:
"Looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith; who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God." The witnesses note the runner looking to Jesus, author and finisher of faith. On "looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith," the grammatical voices note the fixed gaze. Barnes and Robertson note the runner's focus: "looking (aphorontes — looking away from all else, fixing the eyes) unto Jesus the author (archegon — leader, originator, pioneer) and finisher (teleioten — perfecter, completer) of our faith" — the runner keeps his eye fixed on Jesus, who both originates and perfects faith; he is the supreme example and the goal.

On "who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame," the witnesses note his endurance. Barnes and Calvin note that Jesus, "for (in view of, or in exchange for) the joy that was set before him (the future glory and the redemption of his people) endured the cross, despising (disregarding, counting as nothing) the shame" — he bore the cross and scorned its shame for the sake of the joy to come; the supreme pattern of patient endurance.

On "is set down at the right hand of the throne of God," the eastern Cyril notes the reward: he "is set down (enthroned) at the right hand of the throne of God" — his endurance issued in his exaltation; the crown that followed the cross, the encouragement that the race ends in glory. The verse displays the runner looking to Jesus, author and finisher of faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fixed gaze — "looking (looking away from all else) unto Jesus the author (originator) and finisher (perfecter) of our faith," the runner's eye fixed on Jesus, the example and goal (Barnes, Robertson); "for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame," bearing the cross and scorning its shame for the joy to come (Barnes, Calvin); "set down at the right hand of the throne of God," his exaltation crowning his endurance (Cyril). The verse displays the looking. The verse marks the gaze: unto Jesus. The verse displays the office: author and finisher. The verse displays the endurance: the cross, despising shame. The verse displays the reward: enthroned. The looking displays Jesus the pattern and goal of faith. The verse displays the Jesus: looked to. The verse displays the cross: endured. The verse displays the throne: attained. The runner looking to Jesus, author and finisher of faith, displays the focus of the race: fixing the eye on Jesus — who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is now enthroned at God's right hand — the supreme pattern of endurance crowned with glory. The verse displays the enthroned Jesus, whom we consider lest we faint (v.3).
3:
"For consider him that endured such contradiction of sinners against himself, lest ye be wearied and faint in your minds." The brief verse notes the call to consider Christ's endurance, lest we faint. The grammatical voices note the comparison. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation to ponder Christ: "consider (analogisasthe — reckon up, compare, weigh carefully) him that endured such contradiction (antilogian — opposition, hostility, gainsaying) of sinners against himself" — weigh how much Christ endured from the hostility of sinful men.

On "contradiction of sinners against himself," the witnesses note the opposition. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ endured "such contradiction (gainsaying, opposition) of sinners" — the bitter hostility, insults, and opposition of sinful men; far greater than the readers had yet suffered.

On "lest ye be wearied and faint in your minds," the puritan Henry notes the purpose: "lest ye be wearied (grow weary) and faint (ekluomenoi — be loosened, exhausted) in your minds (souls)" — the antidote to spiritual weariness and discouragement is to consider Christ's far greater endurance; comparing our trials with his keeps us from fainting. The verse displays the call to consider Christ's endurance.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exhortation — "consider (weigh carefully) him that endured such contradiction (opposition) of sinners against himself," pondering how much Christ endured from hostile men (Barnes, Robertson); "contradiction of sinners," the bitter hostility far greater than the readers' (Barnes, Calvin); "lest ye be wearied and faint in your minds," considering Christ the antidote to discouragement (Henry). The verse displays the comparison. The verse marks the consideration: weigh Christ. The verse displays the endurance: contradiction of sinners. The verse displays the danger: weariness, fainting. The verse displays the antidote: consider him. The call displays Christ's endurance steadying the weary. The verse displays the considering: urged. The verse displays the contradiction: endured. The verse displays the fainting: prevented. The call to consider Christ's endurance, lest we faint, displays the remedy for discouragement: weigh carefully how Christ endured such hostility of sinners against himself — so that, comparing our lighter trials with his, we be not wearied and faint in our souls. The verse displays Christ's endurance, far beyond the readers' yet-unbloodied striving (v.4).
4:
"Ye have not yet resisted unto blood, striving against sin." The brief verse notes that the readers had not yet resisted to the point of blood. The grammatical voices note the comparison of suffering. Barnes and Robertson note the gentle reproof: "ye have not yet resisted unto blood (to the shedding of blood, to death), striving (antagonizomenoi — contending, as in a contest) against sin" — in their struggle against sin and persecution, the readers had not yet been called to die; their suffering, real as it was, had not reached martyrdom.

On "resisted unto blood," the eastern Chrysostom notes the contrast with Christ. Chrysostom notes that, unlike Christ (who resisted unto blood, shedding his own), and unlike the martyrs of old, the readers had not yet poured out their blood; their trials, though grievous, fell short of the supreme cost.

On "striving against sin," the puritan Henry notes the contest: their "striving against sin" — the conflict against temptation and the sinful opposition of persecutors — was a real contest, but not yet the bloody one; the reminder that they had not suffered the worst should shame them out of fainting. The verse displays that the readers had not yet resisted to the point of blood.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gentle reproof — "ye have not yet resisted unto blood (to death), striving (contending) against sin," the readers not yet called to die, their suffering short of martyrdom (Barnes, Robertson); the contrast with Christ and the martyrs who did shed their blood (Chrysostom); "striving against sin," a real contest but not yet the bloody one, the reminder shaming them out of fainting (Henry). The verse displays the unbloodied striving. The verse marks the resistance: not yet unto blood. The verse displays the striving: against sin. The verse displays the comparison: short of martyrdom. The verse displays the reproof: not the worst. The comparison displays the readers' trials short of death. The verse displays the blood: not yet shed. The verse displays the striving: real. The verse displays the contest: against sin. That the readers had not yet resisted to the point of blood displays the gentle reproof that steadies them: in their striving against sin they had not yet been called to shed their blood, as Christ and the martyrs did — their trials, grievous as they were, fell short of the supreme cost, and so should not make them faint. The verse displays the unbloodied contest, met with the exhortation to endure chastening (vv.5-6).
5:
"And ye have forgotten the exhortation which speaketh unto you as unto children, My son, despise not thou the chastening of the Lord, nor faint when thou art rebuked of him:" The witnesses note the exhortation not to despise the Lord's chastening. On "ye have forgotten the exhortation which speaketh unto you as unto children," the grammatical voices note the appeal to Scripture. Barnes and Robertson note the citation (Proverbs 3:11-12): "ye have forgotten the exhortation (paraklesis — the encouraging word) which speaketh (reasons, addresses) unto you as unto children (sons)" — the readers had forgotten a Scripture that addresses them tenderly as God's sons.

On "despise not thou the chastening of the Lord," the witnesses note the first warning. Barnes and Calvin note the first danger: "despise not (do not make light of, treat with contempt) the chastening (paideia — discipline, training, correction) of the Lord" — not to undervalue God's discipline, as if it were a small thing or a sign of his displeasure to be scorned.

On "nor faint when thou art rebuked of him," the puritan Henry notes the opposite danger: "nor faint (lose heart, be discouraged) when thou art rebuked (corrected) of him" — the other extreme, sinking under discipline in despair; the believer is to avoid both despising and fainting, taking discipline rightly. The verse displays the exhortation not to despise the Lord's chastening.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation (Proverbs 3:11-12) — "the exhortation which speaketh unto you as unto children (sons)," a Scripture addressing them as God's sons, forgotten (Barnes, Robertson); "despise not the chastening (discipline) of the Lord," not to make light of God's correction (Barnes, Calvin); "nor faint when thou art rebuked," not to sink in despair, avoiding both extremes (Henry). The verse displays the exhortation. The verse marks the address: as unto sons. The verse displays the first danger: despising. The verse displays the discipline: of the Lord. The verse displays the second danger: fainting. The exhortation displays the two extremes to avoid in discipline. The verse displays the chastening: not despised. The verse displays the rebuke: not fainted under. The verse displays the sons: addressed. The exhortation not to despise the Lord's chastening displays the forgotten word of Scripture: addressed tenderly as God's sons, the readers are neither to despise the Lord's discipline as a small thing nor to faint under his rebuke in despair. The verse displays the right reception of discipline, grounded in the Father's love (v.6).
6:
"For whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth, and scourgeth every son whom he receiveth." The brief verse notes that the Lord chastens those he loves. The grammatical voices note the principle of love. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of discipline (Proverbs 3:12): "whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth (disciplines), and scourgeth (mastigoi — flogs, applies sharper correction) every son whom he receiveth (accepts as a son)" — God's chastening is the proof, not the contradiction, of his love; he disciplines those he loves as his sons.

On "whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth," the witnesses note the token of sonship. Barnes and Calvin note that chastening is a mark of God's love and of true sonship — far from showing rejection, it shows that God treats the sufferer as a beloved child; the rod is a sign of fatherly care.

On "scourgeth every son whom he receiveth," the puritan Henry notes the universality: God "scourgeth every son" — no true son is exempt; all whom God receives he disciplines; the sharpness of "scourgeth" shows that even severe correction flows from love. The verse displays that the Lord chastens those he loves.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle (Proverbs 3:12) — "whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth, and scourgeth (flogs) every son whom he receiveth," chastening the proof of God's love, not its contradiction (Barnes, Robertson); chastening a mark of love and true sonship, the rod a sign of fatherly care (Barnes, Calvin); "scourgeth every son," no true son exempt, even severe correction flowing from love (Henry). The verse displays the loving chastening. The verse marks the love: whom he loveth. The verse displays the chastening: he disciplines. The verse displays the scourging: every son. The verse displays the reception: as sons. The principle displays God's discipline as the token of love. The verse displays the loving: God. The verse displays the chastening: of sons. The verse displays the reception: into sonship. That the Lord chastens those he loves displays the comforting ground of discipline: God chastens whom he loves and scourges every son he receives — so that correction, far from proving rejection, is the very token of his fatherly love and of true sonship. The verse displays the Father's loving discipline, the sign that we are sons (vv.7-8).
7:
"If ye endure chastening, God dealeth with you as with sons; for what son is he whom the father chasteneth not?" The brief verse notes that chastening proves God deals with us as sons. The grammatical voices note the argument from sonship. Barnes and Robertson note the inference: "if ye endure chastening (bear it patiently), God dealeth with (treats, offers himself to) you as with sons" — the endurance of discipline is evidence that God treats the sufferer as a son; chastening is part of the Father's dealing with his children.

On "God dealeth with you as with sons," the witnesses note the privilege. Barnes and Calvin note that to be chastened is to be dealt with "as sons" — discipline is the mark of the family relationship; God's correction places the believer among his children, not his enemies.

On "what son is he whom the father chasteneth not," the puritan Henry notes the universal experience: "what son is he whom the father chasteneth not?" — every true father disciplines his children; an undisciplined son is unheard of; so God's discipline is the common lot of all his true sons, proving their sonship. The verse displays that chastening proves God deals with us as sons.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inference — "if ye endure chastening, God dealeth with you as with sons," enduring discipline evidence that God treats us as sons (Barnes, Robertson); to be chastened is to be dealt with "as sons," the mark of the family relationship (Barnes, Calvin); "what son is he whom the father chasteneth not?" every true father disciplining his children, an undisciplined son unheard of (Henry). The verse displays the sonship proved. The verse marks the endurance: of chastening. The verse displays the dealing: as with sons. The verse displays the question: what son uncorrected. The verse displays the universality: all sons disciplined. The argument displays chastening as the mark of sonship. The verse displays the chastening: endured. The verse displays the sons: so treated. The verse displays the father: disciplining. That chastening proves God deals with us as sons displays the comfort of the argument: enduring discipline is evidence that God treats us as his children, for every father disciplines his sons — an uncorrected son is unknown; so God's chastening marks us as truly his. The verse displays the discipline of sonship, whose absence would mean we were not sons (v.8).
8:
"But if ye be without chastisement, whereof all are partakers, then are ye bastards, and not sons." The brief verse notes that the lack of chastisement would prove illegitimacy. The grammatical voices note the sharp inference. Barnes and Robertson note the reverse argument: "if ye be without chastisement (discipline), whereof all (all true sons) are partakers, then are ye bastards (nothoi — illegitimate children), and not sons" — to be without discipline would prove one no true son but illegitimate; all genuine sons share in the Father's correction.

On "whereof all are partakers," the witnesses note the universality. Barnes and Calvin note that "all are partakers" of chastisement — every true child of God shares in discipline; it is the common experience of the whole family of God, none excepted.

On "then are ye bastards, and not sons," the puritan Henry notes the solemn conclusion: the absence of discipline would mark one "bastards, and not sons" — illegitimate children, not owned by the father, left without the correction that belongs to true sonship; so the believer should welcome chastening as proof he is a true son. The verse displays that the lack of chastisement would prove illegitimacy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reverse argument — "if ye be without chastisement, whereof all are partakers, then are ye bastards (illegitimate), and not sons," lacking discipline proving one no true son (Barnes, Robertson); "all are partakers," every true child sharing in discipline, none excepted (Barnes, Calvin); "bastards, and not sons," the undisciplined unowned by the father, the believer welcoming chastening as proof of sonship (Henry). The verse displays the test of sonship. The verse marks the lack: without chastisement. The verse displays the universality: all partakers. The verse displays the verdict: bastards not sons. The verse displays the welcome: chastening proves sonship. The inference displays discipline as the badge of legitimate sonship. The verse displays the chastisement: shared by all. The verse displays the bastards: undisciplined. The verse displays the sons: corrected. That the lack of chastisement would prove illegitimacy displays the sharp turn of the argument: since all true sons share in discipline, to be without it would prove one not a son at all but illegitimate — so the believer should welcome chastening as the very badge of true sonship. The verse displays the test of sonship, leading to subjection to the Father of spirits (v.9).
9:
"Furthermore we have had fathers of our flesh which corrected us, and we gave them reverence: shall we not much rather be in subjection unto the Father of spirits, and live?" The witnesses note the argument from earthly fathers to the Father of spirits. On "we have had fathers of our flesh which corrected us, and we gave them reverence," the grammatical voices note the lesser case. Barnes and Robertson note the comparison: "we have had fathers of our flesh (earthly, human fathers) which corrected (disciplined) us, and we gave them reverence (respect, submission)" — we submitted to and respected our human fathers who disciplined us; the lesser and familiar case.

On "shall we not much rather be in subjection unto the Father of spirits," the witnesses note the greater. Barnes and Calvin note the a fortiori: "shall we not much rather be in subjection unto the Father of spirits" — if we submitted to earthly fathers, how much more should we submit to God, "the Father of spirits" (the source of our spiritual being, or of all spirits); the greater Father deserves greater submission.

On "the Father of spirits, and live," the grammatical-historical note the title and the reward: "the Father of spirits" contrasts with "fathers of our flesh" — God is the Father of our higher, spiritual nature; and submission to his discipline issues in life ("and live") — true, spiritual, eternal life. The verse displays the argument from earthly fathers to the Father of spirits.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comparison — "fathers of our flesh (earthly fathers) which corrected us, and we gave them reverence," submitting to human fathers who disciplined us (Barnes, Robertson); "much rather be in subjection unto the Father of spirits," how much more submit to God, the greater Father (Barnes, Calvin); "the Father of spirits" (of our higher nature) contrasted with fathers of the flesh, submission issuing in life (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the greater subjection. The verse marks the earthly: fathers of the flesh. The verse displays the reverence: given them. The verse displays the greater: the Father of spirits. The verse displays the reward: and live. The argument displays greater submission owed to the spiritual Father. The verse displays the fathers: of flesh. The verse displays the subjection: to God. The verse displays the life: gained. The argument from earthly fathers to the Father of spirits displays the a fortiori for submission: if we revered our earthly fathers who disciplined us, how much more should we submit to the Father of our spirits — whose discipline leads not to harm but to life. The verse displays the Father of spirits, whose discipline aims at our profit (v.10).
10:
"For they verily for a few days chastened us after their own pleasure; but he for our profit, that we might be partakers of his holiness." The witnesses note God's discipline aimed at our partaking of his holiness. On "they verily for a few days chastened us after their own pleasure," the grammatical voices note the imperfection of earthly discipline. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "they (earthly fathers) verily for a few days (during childhood, briefly) chastened us after their own pleasure (as seemed good to them, sometimes arbitrarily or imperfectly)" — human discipline was brief and according to fallible human judgment.

On "but he for our profit," the witnesses note God's purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast: "but he (God) for our profit (to benefit us, for our good)" — God's discipline is never arbitrary but always aimed at our true advantage; unlike fallible human correction, his is perfectly wise and purposeful.

On "that we might be partakers of his holiness," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the high aim: God disciplines "that we might be partakers (sharers) of his holiness" — the goal of God's chastening is our sanctification, that we may share in his own holiness; the highest possible end, making affliction a means of grace. The verse displays God's discipline aimed at our partaking of his holiness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "they (earthly fathers) for a few days chastened us after their own pleasure," human discipline brief and according to fallible judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "but he for our profit," God's discipline never arbitrary but for our true good (Barnes, Calvin); "that we might be partakers of his holiness," the high aim of sanctification, sharing God's own holiness (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the purposeful discipline. The verse marks the earthly: for a few days, their pleasure. The verse displays the divine: for our profit. The verse displays the contrast: fallible and wise. The verse displays the aim: his holiness. The contrast displays God's discipline aimed at our sanctification. The verse displays the chastening: human and divine. The verse displays the profit: ours. The verse displays the holiness: shared. God's discipline aimed at our partaking of his holiness displays the surpassing purpose of his correction: earthly fathers disciplined briefly and by fallible judgment, but God always for our profit — that we might share in his own holiness, the highest end. The verse displays the holy aim of discipline, whose fruit is righteousness and peace (v.11).
11:
"Now no chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous, but grievous: nevertheless afterward it yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness unto them which are exercised thereby." The witnesses note the painful discipline yielding the peaceable fruit of righteousness. On "no chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous, but grievous," the grammatical voices note the present pain. Barnes and Robertson note the honest acknowledgment: "no chastening for the present (at the time) seemeth to be joyous, but grievous (painful, sorrowful)" — discipline is not pleasant while it lasts; it is felt as pain, not joy; the writer does not pretend otherwise.

On "nevertheless afterward it yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness," the witnesses note the later harvest. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast: "nevertheless afterward (later, when it has done its work) it yieldeth (gives back, produces) the peaceable fruit of righteousness (the fruit which is righteousness, bringing peace)" — though painful at the time, discipline afterward bears a harvest of righteousness and peace; the bitter root yields sweet fruit.

On "unto them which are exercised thereby," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the condition: this fruit comes "unto them which are exercised (gegymnasmenois — trained, as an athlete by exercise) thereby" — the peaceable fruit belongs to those who are trained by the discipline, who submit and let it do its work; the metaphor of the athlete trained by hard exercise. The verse displays the painful discipline yielding the peaceable fruit of righteousness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the present pain — "no chastening for the present seemeth joyous, but grievous (painful)," discipline felt as pain, not pleasant while it lasts (Barnes, Robertson); "afterward it yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness," the later harvest of righteousness and peace, the bitter root yielding sweet fruit (Barnes, Calvin); "unto them which are exercised (trained, as an athlete) thereby," the fruit belonging to those trained by it (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruit of discipline. The verse marks the present: grievous. The verse displays the afterward: peaceable fruit. The verse displays the fruit: righteousness. The verse displays the condition: trained thereby. The contrast displays painful discipline bearing sweet fruit. The verse displays the chastening: grievous now. The verse displays the fruit: peaceable. The verse displays the exercised: trained. The painful discipline yielding the peaceable fruit of righteousness displays the comfort that completes the argument: no chastening seems joyous at the time but grievous — yet afterward it yields the peaceable fruit of righteousness to those who are trained by it, the bitter present giving way to a harvest of peace. The verse displays the fruit of discipline, calling the weary to lift up their hands (vv.12-13).
12:
"Wherefore lift up the hands which hang down, and the feeble knees;" The brief verse notes the call to renew strength: lift the drooping hands. The grammatical voices note the exhortation to the weary. Barnes and Robertson note the figure (Isaiah 35:3): "lift up (strengthen, raise) the hands which hang down (drooping, slack with discouragement), and the feeble (paralyzed, weakened) knees" — the picture of an exhausted, discouraged runner or traveler; the readers are to rouse themselves from spiritual fatigue.

On "the hands which hang down," the witnesses note the discouragement. Barnes and Calvin note that "hands which hang down" picture flagging zeal and discouragement; the call is to renewed effort, to take fresh courage after the teaching on discipline.

On "the feeble knees," the puritan Henry notes the application: "the feeble knees" (Isaiah 35:3) — weakened by fear or fatigue; the believer, drooping under trial, is to be strengthened and pressed on; the exhortation to mutual encouragement and personal resolve in the race. The verse displays the call to renew strength: lift the drooping hands.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure (Isaiah 35:3) — "lift up (strengthen) the hands which hang down (drooping with discouragement), and the feeble knees," rousing the exhausted runner from spiritual fatigue (Barnes, Robertson); "hands which hang down" picturing flagging zeal, the call to fresh courage (Barnes, Calvin); "the feeble knees" weakened by fear or fatigue, the drooping believer to be strengthened (Henry). The verse displays the renewed strength. The verse marks the hands: hanging down. The verse displays the knees: feeble. The verse displays the call: lift up, strengthen. The verse displays the application: rouse from fatigue. The exhortation displays the weary roused to fresh effort. The verse displays the hands: lifted. The verse displays the knees: strengthened. The verse displays the discouragement: overcome. The call to renew strength — lift the drooping hands — displays the practical exhortation flowing from the teaching on discipline: like an exhausted runner, the discouraged believer is to lift up the hands that hang down and strengthen the feeble knees, rousing himself to fresh effort in the race. The verse displays the renewed strength, making straight paths for the feet (v.13).
13:
"And make straight paths for your feet, lest that which is lame be turned out of the way; but let it rather be healed." The brief verse notes the call to make straight paths, that the lame be healed. The grammatical voices note the smooth path. Barnes and Robertson note the figure (Proverbs 4:26): "make straight (orthas — level, smooth) paths for your feet" — make the way plain and even, removing obstacles; live so that the path is clear, both for oneself and for weaker believers.

On "lest that which is lame be turned out of the way," the witnesses note the weak. Barnes and Calvin note the concern: "lest that which is lame (the weak, halting believer) be turned out of the way (dislocated, or led astray)" — a rough path would cause the lame to stumble and wander; the strong are to smooth the way so the weak are not driven off.

On "but let it rather be healed," the puritan Henry notes the aim: "let it rather be healed (restored, strengthened)" — the goal is not to lose the weak but to heal and restore them; by making the path straight, the lame are helped toward recovery. The verse displays the call to make straight paths, that the lame be healed.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure (Proverbs 4:26) — "make straight (level, smooth) paths for your feet," making the way plain, removing obstacles for self and weaker believers (Barnes, Robertson); "lest that which is lame (the weak believer) be turned out of the way," a rough path causing the weak to stumble and wander (Barnes, Calvin); "let it rather be healed," the goal to restore the weak, not lose them (Henry). The verse displays the straight path. The verse marks the paths: made straight. The verse displays the concern: the lame. The verse displays the danger: turned out of the way. The verse displays the aim: healed. The exhortation displays the way smoothed to heal the weak. The verse displays the paths: level. The verse displays the lame: helped. The verse displays the healing: sought. The call to make straight paths, that the lame be healed, displays the care for the weak that completes the exhortation: by making the way plain and even, the believer keeps the lame from being turned aside, and helps them rather toward healing and restoration. The verse displays the smoothed path, leading to peace and holiness with all (v.14).
14:
"Follow peace with all men, and holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord:" The witnesses note the pursuit of peace and holiness, without which none sees the Lord. On "follow peace with all men," the grammatical voices note the pursuit of peace. Barnes and Robertson note the command: "follow (dioko — pursue, eagerly seek) peace with all men" — actively pursue peaceable relations with everyone, so far as possible; not mere passive avoidance of strife but earnest pursuit of peace.

On "and holiness," the witnesses note the inward pursuit. Barnes and Calvin note that alongside peace, the believer is to pursue "holiness (hagiasmon — sanctification, consecration to God)" — the pursuit of personal holiness goes hand in hand with peace; both are to be earnestly sought.

On "without which no man shall see the Lord," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the solemn condition: "without which (holiness) no man shall see the Lord" — holiness is indispensable; without it none shall see God; the vision of God is reserved for the holy (Matthew 5:8). This is no optional extra but a necessity; without sanctification there is no salvation. The verse displays the pursuit of peace and holiness, without which none sees the Lord.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the pursuit — "follow (pursue, eagerly seek) peace with all men," actively pursuing peaceable relations with all (Barnes, Robertson); "and holiness (sanctification)," pursuing personal holiness alongside peace (Barnes, Calvin); "without which no man shall see the Lord," holiness indispensable, the vision of God reserved for the holy (Matthew 5:8), no salvation without sanctification (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays peace and holiness. The verse marks the peace: pursued with all. The verse displays the holiness: pursued. The verse displays the necessity: without which. The verse displays the vision: none see the Lord. The pursuit displays peace and holiness as indispensable. The verse displays the peace: sought. The verse displays the holiness: required. The verse displays the Lord: seen by the holy. The pursuit of peace and holiness, without which none sees the Lord, displays the twofold duty and its solemn ground: pursue peace with all and the holiness without which no man shall see the Lord — holiness being no optional extra but the indispensable condition of seeing God. The verse displays the necessary holiness, guarded by watchful diligence (v.15).
15:
"Looking diligently lest any man fail of the grace of God; lest any root of bitterness springing up trouble you, and thereby many be defiled;" The witnesses note the watchful diligence against falling and the root of bitterness. On "looking diligently lest any man fail of the grace of God," the grammatical voices note the mutual watch. Barnes and Robertson note the oversight: "looking diligently (episkopountes — exercising oversight, watching carefully) lest any man fail of (fall short of, come short of, miss) the grace of God" — the believers are to watch over one another, lest anyone fall away from or fail to obtain God's grace.

On "lest any root of bitterness springing up trouble you," the witnesses note the corrupting influence. Barnes and Calvin note the figure (Deuteronomy 29:18): "lest any root of bitterness (a bitter root, a person or principle producing bitter fruit — apostasy, sin, or a corrupting individual) springing up trouble (annoy, harass) you" — a single corrupt person or evil principle, like a bitter root, can spring up and cause trouble in the community.

On "and thereby many be defiled," the puritan Henry notes the spreading harm: "thereby many be defiled (polluted, corrupted)" — the bitter root, if not checked, spreads its corruption to many; one apostate or corrupting influence can infect the whole body; hence the need for vigilant mutual oversight. The verse displays the watchful diligence against falling and the root of bitterness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the oversight — "looking diligently (watching carefully) lest any man fail of (fall short of) the grace of God," watching over one another lest any fall away (Barnes, Robertson); "lest any root of bitterness (Deuteronomy 29:18) springing up trouble you," a corrupt person or principle like a bitter root causing trouble (Barnes, Calvin); "thereby many be defiled," the corruption spreading to many if unchecked (Henry). The verse displays the mutual watch. The verse marks the diligence: looking carefully. The verse displays the danger: failing of grace. The verse displays the root: of bitterness. The verse displays the spread: many defiled. The watch displays the community guarding against corruption. The verse displays the looking: diligent. The verse displays the root: bitter. The verse displays the defilement: spreading. The watchful diligence against falling and the root of bitterness displays the call to mutual care: watch carefully lest anyone fall short of God's grace, and lest a bitter root spring up to cause trouble and defile many — vigilant oversight guarding the whole body from corruption. The verse displays the watch against corruption, exemplified in the warning of Esau (v.16).
16:
"Lest there be any fornicator, or profane person, as Esau, who for one morsel of meat sold his birthright." The witnesses note the warning against the profane, as Esau who sold his birthright. On "lest there be any fornicator, or profane person," the grammatical voices note the double warning. Barnes and Robertson note the dangers named: "lest there be any fornicator (one guilty of sexual immorality), or profane person (bebelos — irreligious, worldly, one who treats sacred things as common)" — two evils to guard against: immorality and profanity (the irreverent, godless temper).

On "as Esau," the witnesses note the example. Barnes and Calvin note that Esau is the type of the "profane person" — not necessarily a fornicator, but profane: one who held sacred things cheap, valuing the earthly above the spiritual; the example of irreverent worldliness.

On "who for one morsel of meat sold his birthright," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the profanity displayed: Esau "for one morsel of meat (a single meal, the pottage) sold his birthright (prototokia — the rights of the firstborn, including the spiritual blessing)" — he bartered away his sacred privilege for a momentary appetite; the picture of profanity, preferring the fleeting and physical to the eternal and holy; a warning against those who would sell their spiritual birthright (their share in Christ) for the world's passing pleasures. The verse displays the warning against the profane, as Esau who sold his birthright.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the double warning — "lest there be any fornicator, or profane person (irreligious, worldly)," guarding against immorality and profanity (Barnes, Robertson); "as Esau," the type of the profane, holding sacred things cheap (Barnes, Calvin); "for one morsel of meat sold his birthright," bartering the sacred privilege for a momentary appetite, a warning against selling the spiritual for the fleeting (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the evils: fornicator, profane. The verse displays the example: Esau. The verse displays the act: sold his birthright. The verse displays the price: one morsel. The warning displays Esau's profane bartering of the sacred. The verse displays the profane: as Esau. The verse displays the birthright: sold. The verse displays the morsel: the price. The warning against the profane, as Esau who sold his birthright, displays the danger held up to the readers: let none be immoral or profane like Esau, who for a single meal sold his birthright — preferring a momentary appetite to his sacred privilege, the very image of those who would barter their spiritual inheritance for the world. The verse displays Esau's profanity, whose irretrievable loss is the warning (v.17).
17:
"For ye know how that afterward, when he would have inherited the blessing, he was rejected: for he found no place of repentance, though he sought it carefully with tears." The witnesses note Esau rejected, finding no place of repentance — the crux of whose repentance. On "afterward, when he would have inherited the blessing, he was rejected," the grammatical voices note the irretrievable loss. Barnes and Robertson note the outcome (Genesis 27): "afterward, when he would have inherited the blessing (the father's blessing, sought too late), he was rejected (apedokimasthe — disapproved, refused)" — Esau, having sold his birthright, later sought the blessing but was refused; the loss could not be undone.

On "he found no place of repentance," interpreters divide over whose repentance. The grammatical-historical Clarke (strongly) and Barnes (leaning), with Beza, Doddridge, and Stuart, take "repentance" as a change of mind in Isaac — Esau "found no place of repentance" meaning he could not bring his father to reverse the blessing once pronounced; the father's mind could not be changed. The Reformed Calvin (strongly), with Wesley and Ellicott's main reading, take it as Esau's own repentance — he "found no room for repentance," i.e., his late repentance availed nothing to regain the lost blessing; the door was closed, his tears too late and not the tears of true conversion but of one past hope.

On "though he sought it carefully with tears," the witnesses note the vain seeking. All agree Esau "sought it (the blessing) carefully (earnestly) with tears" but in vain — whether seeking to change Isaac's mind or to recover by repentance what he had lost. All also agree the verse does not teach that a penitent sinner cannot be received (Barnes explicitly rejects that misuse); it warns against despising one's privileges till the loss is irretrievable. The verse displays Esau rejected, finding no place of repentance.

The witnesses agree the substance: Esau, having sold his birthright, was rejected when he later sought the blessing — his loss irretrievable, his tears unavailing; the warning is against trifling with sacred privileges till they are lost beyond recovery, not against the penitence of a true seeker (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley). But whose "repentance" divides them: Clarke and Barnes (with Beza, Doddridge, Stuart) take it as Isaac's change of mind, which Esau could not procure; Calvin, Wesley, and Ellicott take it as Esau's own repentance, which found no room to regain the lost blessing. The verse displays the repentance crux. The verse marks the rejection: he was refused. The verse displays the debate: Isaac's mind or Esau's repentance. The verse displays the seeking: with tears. The verse displays the loss: irretrievable. The crux displays Esau's vain seeking of the forfeited blessing. The verse displays the blessing: lost. The verse displays the repentance: no place. The verse displays the tears: too late. Esau rejected, finding no place of repentance, displays the solemn warning and a famous crux: having sold his birthright, Esau was refused the blessing and found no place of repentance — read as no change of mind in Isaac, whom he could not move (Clarke, Barnes, Beza), or as no room for his own repentance to regain what he had lost (Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott) — though he sought it with tears; the warning being against forfeiting sacred privilege beyond recovery. The verse displays the irretrievable loss, contrasted with the readers' access to grace (vv.18-24).
18:
"For ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched, and that burned with fire, nor unto blackness, and darkness, and tempest," The witnesses note the contrast: not come to the terrors of Sinai. On "ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched," the grammatical voices note the earthly mountain. Barnes and Robertson note the description of Sinai: "ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched (a material, tangible mountain, Sinai), and that burned with fire" — the readers have not come, as Israel did, to the physical Mount Sinai, ablaze with fire at the giving of the law (Exodus 19).

On "blackness, and darkness, and tempest," the witnesses note the terrors. Barnes and Calvin note the dreadful phenomena: "blackness (gloom), and darkness, and tempest (storm, whirlwind)" — the awful, terrifying manifestations that accompanied the law's giving at Sinai; the scene of terror and dread.

On the contrast, the eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the design: the writer recalls Sinai's terrors (vv.18-21) precisely to contrast them with the gracious Mount Zion (vv.22-24) to which believers have come; the old covenant came in terror, the new in grace. The verse displays the contrast: not come to the terrors of Sinai.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the earthly mountain — "ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched (material Sinai), and that burned with fire," not come to the physical Sinai ablaze at the law's giving (Exodus 19, Barnes, Robertson); "blackness, and darkness, and tempest," the terrifying phenomena of the law's giving (Barnes, Calvin); the terrors recalled to contrast with the gracious Zion to come, old covenant in terror, new in grace (Chrysostom, Henry). The verse displays the terrors of Sinai. The verse marks the mount: that might be touched. The verse displays the fire: burning. The verse displays the terrors: blackness, darkness, tempest. The verse displays the contrast: not come here. The scene displays the dreadful Sinai of the law. The verse displays the mount: material. The verse displays the fire: blazing. The verse displays the tempest: dreadful. The contrast — not come to the terrors of Sinai — displays the writer's design: believers have not come to the tangible mountain that burned with fire, to blackness, darkness, and tempest — the dreadful scene of the law's giving — but, as the contrast will show, to something far better. The verse displays the terrors of the old covenant, with its fearful voice (vv.19-21).
19:
"And the sound of a trumpet, and the voice of words; which voice they that heard intreated that the word should not be spoken to them any more:" The brief verse notes the fearful trumpet and voice at Sinai. The grammatical voices note the awful sounds. Barnes and Robertson note the continuation of the Sinai scene: "the sound of a trumpet (the loud trumpet-blast, Exodus 19:16,19), and the voice of words (the spoken words of God, the Decalogue)" — the terrifying trumpet and the very voice of God speaking the law added to the dread.

On "the voice of words," the witnesses note the divine speech. Barnes and Calvin note that "the voice of words" was the articulate voice of God uttering the commandments; so awful that the people could not bear it.

On "intreated that the word should not be spoken to them any more," the puritan Henry notes the people's terror: those who heard "intreated (begged) that the word should not be spoken to them any more" — overwhelmed with dread, the people pleaded that God would not continue to speak directly to them (Exodus 20:19); they could not endure the immediate voice of God. The verse displays the fearful trumpet and voice at Sinai.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the awful sounds — "the sound of a trumpet (the loud blast, Exodus 19), and the voice of words (God speaking the law)," the terrifying trumpet and divine voice (Barnes, Robertson); "the voice of words" the articulate voice of God uttering the commandments, unbearable (Barnes, Calvin); "intreated that the word should not be spoken... any more," the people begging God not to speak directly, overwhelmed with dread (Exodus 20:19, Henry). The verse displays the fearful sounds. The verse marks the trumpet: sounding. The verse displays the voice: of words. The verse displays the terror: unbearable. The verse displays the plea: speak no more. The scene displays the people overwhelmed by God's voice. The verse displays the trumpet: loud. The verse displays the voice: divine. The verse displays the entreaty: to cease. The fearful trumpet and voice at Sinai display the dread of the old covenant's giving: the loud trumpet-blast and the very voice of God speaking the law so terrified the hearers that they begged the word might not be spoken to them any more. The verse displays the unbearable voice, before which even Moses trembled (vv.20-21).
20:
"(For they could not endure that which was commanded, And if so much as a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned, or thrust through with a dart:" The brief verse notes the unbearable command barring the mountain. The grammatical voices note the severity. Barnes and Robertson note the reason for the people's terror: "they could not endure (bear) that which was commanded" — the command was so severe that they could not bear it; even the conditions of approach were terrifying.

On "if so much as a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned," the witnesses note the prohibition. Barnes and Ellicott note the command (Exodus 19:12-13): "if so much as a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned, or thrust through with a dart (a weapon)" — so holy and forbidden was the mountain that even an animal straying onto it must be killed, not touched by hand; the absolute barrier marking God's unapproachable holiness under the law.

On the severity, the grammatical-historical note the contrast with grace: this dreadful exclusion — death even for a beast that touched the mount — measures the distance the law kept between God and man; under the old covenant, approach to God was barred by terror, the very opposite of the access believers now have (vv.22-24). The verse displays the unbearable command barring the mountain.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the severity — "they could not endure (bear) that which was commanded," the command too severe to bear (Barnes, Robertson); "if so much as a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned" (Exodus 19:12-13), the mountain so forbidden even a straying animal must be killed (Barnes, Ellicott); the dreadful exclusion measuring the law's distance between God and man, the opposite of gospel access (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the barred mountain. The verse marks the command: unbearable. The verse displays the prohibition: beast stoned. The verse displays the holiness: unapproachable. The verse displays the contrast: barred by terror. The command displays the law's dreadful barrier around God. The verse displays the beast: stoned. The verse displays the mountain: forbidden. The verse displays the terror: of approach. The unbearable command barring the mountain displays the law's dreadful holiness: the people could not endure the command that even a beast touching the mountain must be stoned — the absolute barrier measuring the distance the old covenant kept between God and man. The verse displays the forbidden mountain, before which Moses himself quaked (v.21).
21:
"And so terrible was the sight, that Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake:)" The brief verse notes that even Moses trembled at the terrible sight. The grammatical voices note the mediator's fear. Barnes and Robertson note the climax of the Sinai terrors: "so terrible (fearful, dreadful) was the sight (the phantazomenon — the appearance, the spectacle), that Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake (tremble)" — even Moses, the mediator of the old covenant, was overcome with fear and trembling at the awful scene.

On "Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Ellicott note that this saying of Moses is not recorded in the Exodus account of Sinai (it may draw on Deuteronomy 9:19 or Jewish tradition); the point stands that even the great lawgiver trembled.

On the force, the eastern Cyril notes the contrast: if even Moses, the mediator who drew nearest to God, "exceedingly feared and quaked," how terrible was the law's giving — and how great, by contrast, the grace and confidence of those who come to Mount Zion (vv.22-24), where the access is one of joy, not dread. The verse displays that even Moses trembled at the terrible sight.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mediator's fear — "so terrible was the sight, that Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake," even Moses overcome with fear at the awful scene (Barnes, Robertson); the saying not in the Exodus account, drawing perhaps on Deuteronomy 9:19 or tradition, but the point clear (Barnes, Ellicott); if even the nearest mediator trembled, how terrible the law, how great the contrast with Zion's grace (Cyril). The verse displays Moses' terror. The verse marks the sight: terrible. The verse displays the mediator: Moses. The verse displays the fear: exceedingly. The verse displays the trembling: quaked. The scene displays even Moses overcome at Sinai. The verse displays the sight: dreadful. The verse displays the Moses: trembling. The verse displays the contrast: with Zion. That even Moses trembled at the terrible sight displays the crown of the Sinai terrors: so dreadful was the scene that even Moses, the mediator, said, "I exceedingly fear and quake" — if the nearest to God trembled, how fearful the law, and how great the grace of those who come instead to Zion. The verse displays the trembling mediator, set against the joyful access to Zion (vv.22-24).
22:
"But ye are come unto mount Sion, and unto the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to an innumerable company of angels," The witnesses note the believers come to Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem. On "ye are come unto mount Sion, and unto the city of the living God," the grammatical voices note the gracious contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the turn from Sinai to Zion: "ye are come (have come, by faith) unto mount Sion (the symbol of grace and the gospel, over against Sinai of the law), and unto the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem" — believers have come not to the terrors of Sinai but to Zion, the city of God, the heavenly Jerusalem; the dwelling-place of grace.

On "the heavenly Jerusalem," the witnesses note the city above. Barnes and Calvin note that "the heavenly Jerusalem" is the true city of God (cf. 11:10,16; Galatians 4:26; Revelation 21) — the eternal, heavenly dwelling, the home of the redeemed, into whose fellowship believers have already entered by faith.

On "an innumerable company of angels," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the angelic host: believers are come "to an innumerable company (myriads, tens of thousands) of angels" — the vast festal assembly of the holy angels; instead of Sinai's terror, the believer joins the joyful gathering of the heavenly host. The verse displays the believers come to Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gracious contrast — "ye are come unto mount Sion (the symbol of grace), and unto the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem," believers come not to Sinai's terror but to Zion, the city of God (Barnes, Robertson); "the heavenly Jerusalem," the true, eternal city of God, home of the redeemed (11:10,16; Galatians 4:26, Barnes, Calvin); "an innumerable company of angels," the vast festal assembly of the holy angels (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the heavenly city. The verse marks the mount: Sion. The verse displays the city: of the living God. The verse displays the Jerusalem: heavenly. The verse displays the host: innumerable angels. The coming displays believers entering the city of grace. The verse displays the Sion: reached. The verse displays the city: heavenly. The verse displays the angels: myriads. The believers come to Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem, display the glorious contrast with Sinai: believers have come, not to the trembling mount of the law, but to Mount Zion, the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to the innumerable festal host of angels. The verse displays the heavenly city, with its assembly of the firstborn and the perfected (v.23).
23:
"To the general assembly and church of the firstborn, which are written in heaven, and to God the Judge of all, and to the spirits of just men made perfect," The witnesses note the heavenly assembly: the firstborn, God the Judge, and the perfected spirits. On "the general assembly and church of the firstborn, which are written in heaven," the grammatical voices note the festal gathering. Barnes and Robertson note the imagery: "the general assembly (panegyris — a festal convocation, a joyful gathering) and church (ekklesia) of the firstborn (prototokon — those of highest privilege), which are written (enrolled, registered as citizens) in heaven" — the joyful assembly of the redeemed, the "firstborn" (all believers as God's privileged people, or the most eminent saints), enrolled as citizens of heaven.

On "God the Judge of all," the witnesses note the solemn name. Barnes and Calvin note that believers are come "to God the Judge of all" — the one to whom all must give account; the name introduced solemnly amid the description of privilege, to inspire reverence; yet he is the Judge to whom believers come, now propitious to them.

On "the spirits of just men made perfect," the grammatical-historical note the perfected saints: "the spirits of just men made perfect (teteleiomenon — perfected, freed from sin, glorified)" — the souls of the righteous, now perfected in heaven; whether the OT saints specifically (Wesley distinguishes them) or all the glorified righteous, they are the departed saints with whom believers are now joined in fellowship. The verse displays the heavenly assembly: the firstborn, God the Judge, and the perfected spirits.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the festal gathering — "the general assembly (festal convocation) and church of the firstborn, which are written (enrolled) in heaven," the joyful assembly of the redeemed, enrolled as heaven's citizens (Barnes, Robertson); "God the Judge of all," the one to whom all account, named to inspire reverence, yet propitious to believers (Barnes, Calvin); "the spirits of just men made perfect," the souls of the righteous now perfected and glorified, with whom believers are joined (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the heavenly company. The verse marks the assembly: of the firstborn. The verse displays the enrollment: in heaven. The verse displays the Judge: God of all. The verse displays the spirits: made perfect. The company displays the redeemed, the Judge, and the perfected. The verse displays the firstborn: enrolled. The verse displays the Judge: reverenced. The verse displays the spirits: perfected. The heavenly assembly — the firstborn, God the Judge, and the perfected spirits — displays the glorious fellowship believers have entered: the festal assembly and church of the firstborn enrolled in heaven, God the Judge of all, and the spirits of the just made perfect. The verse displays the heavenly company, crowned by Jesus the mediator and his sprinkled blood (v.24).
24:
"And to Jesus the mediator of the new covenant, and to the blood of sprinkling, that speaketh better things than that of Abel." The witnesses note Jesus the mediator and his blood that speaks better than Abel's. On "Jesus the mediator of the new covenant," the grammatical voices note the crowning name. Barnes and Robertson note the climax of the things believers have come to: "Jesus the mediator (mesites — go-between, reconciler) of the new (neas — fresh, recent) covenant" — supremely, believers have come to Jesus himself, the mediator of the new covenant; he is the crown of the heavenly assembly.

On "the blood of sprinkling," the witnesses note the atoning blood. Barnes and Calvin note that believers come "to the blood of sprinkling (rhantismou — the sprinkled blood, recalling the old covenant's blood-sprinkling, ch. 9)" — the blood of Christ, applied to the conscience, ratifying the new covenant and cleansing from sin.

On "that speaketh better things than that of Abel," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the contrast with Abel. Barnes notes the comparison: Christ's blood "speaketh better things than that of Abel" — Abel's blood cried out for vengeance (Genesis 4:10), but Christ's blood speaks of pardon, reconciliation, and mercy; (some take "that of Abel" as Abel's own righteous blood crying for justice, others as Abel's sacrifice, but the dominant sense is Abel's blood crying for vengeance vs Christ's blood pleading mercy). The verse displays Jesus the mediator and his blood that speaks better than Abel's.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the crowning name — "Jesus the mediator (reconciler) of the new covenant," supremely come to Jesus, the crown of the assembly (Barnes, Robertson); "the blood of sprinkling," Christ's blood applied to the conscience, ratifying the covenant and cleansing (Barnes, Calvin); "speaketh better things than that of Abel," Christ's blood pleading mercy where Abel's cried for vengeance (Genesis 4:10, Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mediator and his blood. The verse marks the mediator: Jesus. The verse displays the covenant: new. The verse displays the blood: of sprinkling. The verse displays the contrast: better than Abel's. The climax displays Christ's blood speaking mercy. The verse displays the Jesus: mediator. The verse displays the blood: sprinkled. The verse displays the speaking: better. Jesus the mediator and his blood that speaks better than Abel's display the crown of all believers have come to: Jesus himself, mediator of the new covenant, and his sprinkled blood — which, unlike Abel's blood that cried for vengeance, speaks of pardon, reconciliation, and mercy. The verse displays the speaking blood of Christ, demanding that we refuse not him who speaks (v.25).
25:
"See that ye refuse not him that speaketh. For if they escaped not who refused him that spake on earth, much more shall not we escape, if we turn away from him that speaketh from heaven:" The witnesses note the warning not to refuse him who speaks from heaven. On "see that ye refuse not him that speaketh," the grammatical voices note the urgent warning. Barnes and Robertson note the application of the contrast: "see (take heed) that ye refuse not (do not reject, turn a deaf ear to) him that speaketh" — the God who speaks in the gospel (or Christ, whose blood speaks, v.24); the readers must not reject his voice.

On "if they escaped not who refused him that spake on earth," the witnesses note the lesser case. Barnes and Calvin note the argument from lesser to greater: "if they (Israel) escaped not (did not go unpunished) who refused him that spake on earth (God speaking at Sinai through Moses, the earthly mountain)" — those who rejected the voice at Sinai were punished; the law's rejecters did not escape.

On "much more shall not we escape, if we turn away from him that speaketh from heaven," the puritan Henry notes the greater accountability: "much more shall not we escape, if we turn away from him that speaketh from heaven" — if rejecting the earthly voice brought punishment, far more will rejecting the heavenly voice of the gospel; the greater the revelation, the greater the guilt of refusing it. The verse displays the warning not to refuse him who speaks from heaven.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the warning — "see that ye refuse not (reject) him that speaketh," not rejecting the God who speaks in the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); "if they escaped not who refused him that spake on earth (Sinai)," Israel's rejecters punished, not escaping (Barnes, Calvin); "much more shall not we escape, if we turn away from him that speaketh from heaven," far greater guilt in rejecting the heavenly gospel voice, the greater revelation the greater accountability (Henry). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the command: refuse not. The verse displays the speaker: him who speaks. The verse displays the lesser: refused on earth. The verse displays the greater: from heaven. The warning displays the peril of rejecting the heavenly voice. The verse displays the refusing: forbidden. The verse displays the earthly: punished. The verse displays the heavenly: greater. The warning not to refuse him who speaks from heaven displays the application of the whole contrast: take heed not to reject the God who speaks — for if those who refused the earthly voice at Sinai did not escape, much less shall we who turn away from him who speaks from heaven in the gospel. The verse displays the heavenly voice, whose final shaking removes all that can be shaken (vv.26-27).
26:
"Whose voice then shook the earth: but now he hath promised, saying, Yet once more I shake not the earth only, but also heaven." The brief verse notes the promised final shaking of earth and heaven. The grammatical voices note the two shakings. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast of past and future: "whose voice then (at Sinai) shook the earth (the earthquake at the law's giving, Exodus 19:18): but now he hath promised (Haggai 2:6), saying, Yet once more I shake not the earth only, but also heaven" — God's voice once shook the earth at Sinai, but he promises a future, greater shaking of both earth and heaven.

On "whose voice then shook the earth," the witnesses note the past. Barnes and Calvin note that at Sinai God's voice "shook the earth" — a literal trembling of the mountain and ground; the manifestation of his terrible majesty in giving the law.

On "Yet once more I shake not the earth only, but also heaven," the grammatical-historical note the prophecy (Haggai 2:6): "yet once more I shake... earth... also heaven" — a final, universal shaking is promised, embracing heaven as well as earth; the great convulsion that attends the new order and the final consummation, far surpassing the local shaking at Sinai. The verse displays the promised final shaking of earth and heaven.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the two shakings — "whose voice then (at Sinai) shook the earth (the earthquake at the law's giving): but now he hath promised (Haggai 2:6)... Yet once more I shake... earth... also heaven," the past shaking of earth and the promised future shaking of both (Barnes, Robertson); at Sinai God's voice literally shook the earth, manifesting his majesty (Barnes, Calvin); the promised final, universal shaking embracing heaven, the great consummation surpassing Sinai (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the two shakings. The verse marks the past: shook the earth. The verse displays the promise: yet once more. The verse displays the scope: earth and heaven. The verse displays the finality: once more. The prophecy displays the coming universal shaking. The verse displays the voice: shaking. The verse displays the earth: shaken. The verse displays the heaven: also. The promised final shaking of earth and heaven displays the contrast of Sinai and the consummation: God's voice once shook the earth at the law's giving, but he has promised yet one final shaking — of heaven as well as earth — the universal convulsion of the last days. The verse displays the final shaking, which removes the shakable and leaves the unshakable (v.27).
27:
"And this word, Yet once more, signifieth the removing of those things that are shaken, as of things that are made, that those things which cannot be shaken may remain." The witnesses note that the final shaking removes the temporary, leaving the unshakable. On "this word, Yet once more, signifieth the removing of those things that are shaken," the grammatical voices note the meaning of the phrase. Barnes and Robertson note the interpretation of Haggai's words: "this word, Yet once more, signifieth (indicates) the removing (metathesin — removal, change) of those things that are shaken (the shakable, temporary things)" — the very phrase "once more" implies a final removal of all that can be shaken, after which no further shaking is needed.

On "as of things that are made," the witnesses note the created order. Barnes and Calvin note that the things removed are "things that are made (created, temporal things)" — the visible, created order, including the old covenant system; what is made is shakable and passes; the temporary gives way.

On "that those things which cannot be shaken may remain," the grammatical-historical note the abiding kingdom: the purpose is "that those things which cannot be shaken (the unshakable — the kingdom of God, the eternal order) may remain (abide permanently)" — the shaking removes the temporary precisely so that the eternal, unshakable realities may stand forever; the new covenant kingdom abides when all else passes. The verse displays that the final shaking removes the temporary, leaving the unshakable.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the meaning — "this word, Yet once more, signifieth the removing (removal) of those things that are shaken," the phrase implying a final removal of all shakable things (Barnes, Robertson); "things that are made (created, temporal)," the visible created order and old system, shakable and passing (Barnes, Calvin); "that those things which cannot be shaken (the eternal kingdom) may remain," the shaking removing the temporary so the eternal may abide forever (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the removal and the remaining. The verse marks the phrase: yet once more. The verse displays the removing: the shaken. The verse displays the made: temporal. The verse displays the remaining: the unshakable. The shaking displays the temporary removed, the eternal abiding. The verse displays the shaken: removed. The verse displays the made: passing. The verse displays the unshaken: remaining. That the final shaking removes the temporary, leaving the unshakable, displays the purpose of the great convulsion: the phrase "yet once more" signifies the removal of all created, shakable things — so that the things which cannot be shaken, the eternal kingdom of God, may remain forever. The verse displays the unshakable kingdom, which calls for grateful, reverent service (v.28).
28:
"Wherefore we receiving a kingdom which cannot be moved, let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear:" The witnesses note the unshakable kingdom received, calling for reverent, acceptable service. On "we receiving a kingdom which cannot be moved," the grammatical voices note the gift. Barnes and Robertson note the believer's possession: "we receiving (as a gift, being given) a kingdom which cannot be moved (unshakable, the eternal kingdom of God that remains when all else is shaken)" — believers are heirs of the one kingdom that endures the final shaking; the unshakable realm is theirs.

On "let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably," the witnesses note the response. Barnes and Calvin note the exhortation: "let us have grace (echomen charin — let us have/hold grace, or be thankful, gratitude), whereby we may serve (latreuomen — worship, render sacred service to) God acceptably (in a way well-pleasing to him)" — the proper response to receiving the unshakable kingdom is grateful, acceptable worship and service; some render "let us be thankful," others "let us hold fast grace."

On "with reverence and godly fear," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the manner: such service is to be rendered "with reverence (aidous — modesty, awe) and godly fear (eulabeias — reverent caution, holy fear)" — not with terror as at Sinai, but with reverent awe befitting the holy God; grace does not abolish reverence but deepens it. The verse displays the unshakable kingdom received, calling for reverent, acceptable service.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gift — "we receiving a kingdom which cannot be moved (unshakable)," believers heirs of the kingdom that endures the shaking (Barnes, Robertson); "let us have grace (or be thankful), whereby we may serve (worship) God acceptably," grateful, well-pleasing service the response (Barnes, Calvin); "with reverence and godly fear," reverent awe befitting the holy God, grace deepening not abolishing reverence (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the response. The verse marks the kingdom: unshakable. The verse displays the receiving: as a gift. The verse displays the response: serve acceptably. The verse displays the manner: reverence and godly fear. The gift displays the unshakable kingdom calling for reverent service. The verse displays the kingdom: unmoved. The verse displays the grace: held. The verse displays the reverence: due. The unshakable kingdom received, calling for reverent, acceptable service, displays the exhortation flowing from the final shaking: since we receive a kingdom that cannot be moved, let us have grace and gratitude, serving God acceptably with reverence and godly fear. The verse displays the reverent service, grounded in the awful holiness of God (v.29).
29:
"For our God is a consuming fire." The brief verse notes the ground of reverence: our God is a consuming fire. The grammatical voices note the solemn close. Barnes and Robertson note the reason for reverent fear (Deuteronomy 4:24): "for our God is a consuming fire (pyr katanaliskon — a fire that consumes, devours)" — the reason for serving with reverence and godly fear is that God himself is a consuming fire; his holiness consumes all that is unholy.

On "a consuming fire," the witnesses note the holy majesty. Barnes and Calvin note that the figure of "consuming fire" expresses God's awful holiness and justice — terrible against sin and the impenitent; the same God of grace is also a God of consuming holiness, not to be trifled with.

On "our God," the grammatical-historical note the relationship and warning: it is "our God" — the believer's own God, the God of grace — who is yet a consuming fire; the very God who gives the unshakable kingdom is to be approached with awe, for he will consume those who despise his grace. The verse displays the ground of reverence: our God is a consuming fire.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the solemn close (Deuteronomy 4:24) — "our God is a consuming fire (a fire that devours)," the reason for reverent fear, God's holiness consuming the unholy (Barnes, Robertson); "consuming fire" expressing God's awful holiness and justice, terrible against sin, not to be trifled with (Barnes, Calvin); "our God" — the believer's own God of grace yet a consuming fire, to be approached with awe (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the consuming fire. The verse marks the God: ours. The verse displays the fire: consuming. The verse displays the holiness: awful. The verse displays the warning: reverent fear. The close displays the God of grace as consuming holiness. The verse displays the God: a fire. The verse displays the consuming: of the unholy. The verse displays the reverence: grounded. The ground of reverence — our God is a consuming fire — displays the solemn note on which the chapter closes: we are to serve with reverence and godly fear because our God, the very God of grace who gives the unshakable kingdom, is a consuming fire — awful in holiness, not to be despised. The verse displays the consuming fire of God, closing the great chapter on the race of faith, divine discipline, and the unshakable kingdom.
